questions.prompt: Python sometimes makes `is` and `==` appear to agree. What different questions are those operators asking?

answers.text: `==` asks the objects whether their values should count as equal.

feedback.legit: `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: `==` checks value equality as defined by the objects. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Interning can make small demos misleading, but identity and equality remain separate concepts.

feedback.legit: Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Identity and equality are different even when interning sometimes makes examples look similar. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: `is` asks whether both names point to the exact same object.

feedback.legit: `is` checks object identity. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: `is` checks object identity. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: After `b = a` with a list, changing `b` also changed `a`. Did assignment copy anything?

answers.text: No copy happened, so a mutation through `b` is visible when you inspect the same list through `a`.

feedback.legit: A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: A mutation through `b` is visible through `a`. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Both names now refer to one list object. The assignment copied the reference, not the list contents.

feedback.legit: Both names refer to the same list object. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Both names refer to the same list object. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: Python makes an independent list automatically whenever one list variable is assigned to another.

feedback.legit: Assignment binds another name to the same object; copying must be requested. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Assignment binds another name to the same object; copying must be requested. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: Does `range(1_000_000)` allocate a list containing a million Python integers right away?

answers.text: Yes. `range` is only a shorter spelling for constructing the full integer list in memory.

feedback.legit: A range stores compact start, stop, and step information. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: A range stores compact start, stop, and step information. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Its values are produced as iteration needs them, so it does not store that million-element list.

feedback.legit: Range values are produced as needed during iteration. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Range values are produced as needed during iteration. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: You can also use floating-point start, stop, and step values. `range` accepts any numeric type.

feedback.legit: The built-in range requires integer arguments or objects with an integer index interpretation. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: The built-in range requires integer arguments or objects with an integer index interpretation. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: Why does a function with `items=[]` sometimes remember data from an earlier call?

answers.text: Repeated calls can reuse the same default list object instead of creating a fresh one each time.

feedback.legit: Repeated calls can share the same mutable default object. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Repeated calls can share the same mutable default object. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: Default to `None`, then create the list inside the function when you need independent state.

feedback.legit: Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Using `None` and creating the list inside the function avoids shared state. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: The default object is created once when Python defines the function, not once per invocation.

feedback.legit: The default object is created once when the function is defined. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: The default object is created once when the function is defined. The reply belongs under Legit.

questions.prompt: On a normal GIL-enabled CPython build, what can threads run concurrently, and why can I/O-heavy code still benefit?

answers.text: Free-threaded CPython builds are a different configuration and can run with the GIL disabled.

feedback.legit: Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Free-threaded CPython builds can run with the GIL disabled. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Within one GIL-enabled interpreter, only one thread normally executes Python bytecode at a time.

feedback.legit: Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Only one thread normally executes Python bytecode in an interpreter at a time. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Threads can never help I/O-bound code. The GIL blocks every thread even while another one waits on the network.

feedback.legit: Other threads can run while one waits for I/O, and extensions may release the GIL. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Other threads can run while one waits for I/O, and extensions may release the GIL. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

questions.prompt: What do I gain and give up when a Python function yields values instead of returning a completed list?

answers.text: The generator produces values lazily as the caller iterates, which can avoid storing the whole result.

feedback.legit: It produces values lazily as it is iterated. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: It produces values lazily as it is iterated. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: After exhaustion it restarts from the beginning automatically. Every generator is permanently reusable.

feedback.legit: A generator iterator is normally exhausted after one pass. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A generator iterator is normally exhausted after one pass. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: `len(generator)` works because the generator already knows every value it will produce.

feedback.legit: A generator produces values lazily and does not generally provide a length. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: A generator produces values lazily and does not generally provide a length. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: Modern Python dictionaries preserve an order. Is it sorted-key order or the order keys were inserted?

answers.text: They continuously sort keys by value, so iteration order changes whenever a smaller key is inserted.

feedback.legit: Insertion order is preserved, but keys are not automatically sorted. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Insertion order is preserved, but keys are not automatically sorted. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Updating the value of an existing key does not move that key to the end.

feedback.legit: Updating an existing key does not move it to the end. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Updating an existing key does not move it to the end. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Iteration preserves insertion order. The guarantee is not automatic alphabetical or numeric sorting.

feedback.legit: They preserve insertion order. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: They preserve insertion order. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: What happens to every other asyncio task if one coroutine calls blocking code directly?

answers.text: An `await` yields only when the operation and awaited object cooperate with the event loop.

feedback.legit: `await` only yields when the awaited operation cooperates with the event loop. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: `await` only yields when the awaited operation cooperates with the event loop. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Blocking inside the event-loop thread can stall the loop and delay unrelated tasks.

feedback.legit: It can block the event loop and delay other tasks. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: It can block the event loop and delay other tasks. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Asyncio detects every blocking function and transparently moves it to a worker thread.

feedback.legit: Blocking work must be explicitly offloaded or replaced with async I/O. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Blocking work must be explicitly offloaded or replaced with async I/O. That was correctly classified as Sus.

questions.prompt: What contract does a context manager provide around a `with` block, especially when the block raises?

answers.text: It defines entry and exit behavior so setup and cleanup live around the block in one protocol.

feedback.legit: It defines setup and cleanup around a `with` block. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: It defines setup and cleanup around a `with` block. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Context managers are a file-only feature. Locks, transactions, and temporary resources need different syntax.

feedback.legit: Locks, transactions, temporary resources, and custom objects can use the protocol. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Locks, transactions, temporary resources, and custom objects can use the protocol. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Every exception raised inside `with` is suppressed automatically. Cleanup and suppression are inseparable.

feedback.legit: An exception is suppressed only when the exit method explicitly returns a true value. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: An exception is suppressed only when the exit method explicitly returns a true value. Your judgment caught the technical error.

questions.prompt: Properties, bound methods, and managed attributes share the descriptor protocol. What access can a descriptor customize?

answers.text: Descriptors only alter dictionary-key lookup. Attribute access never invokes them.

feedback.legit: Descriptors participate in object attribute access, not normal mapping lookup. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Descriptors participate in object attribute access, not normal mapping lookup. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Python's `property` is implemented through the descriptor protocol, which is why access can execute code.

feedback.legit: Properties are implemented using the descriptor protocol. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Properties are implemented using the descriptor protocol. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Methods such as `__get__`, `__set__`, and `__delete__` can customize attribute access behavior.

feedback.legit: It can customize attribute access through methods such as `__get__` and `__set__`. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: It can customize attribute access through methods such as `__get__` and `__set__`. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: With multiple inheritance, how does Python decide which implementation a method call or `super()` reaches next?

answers.text: Zero-argument `super()` follows the class's method resolution order rather than jumping to one hard-coded parent.

feedback.legit: Zero-argument `super()` follows the MRO rather than naming one fixed parent. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: Zero-argument `super()` follows the MRO rather than naming one fixed parent. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: The MRO provides one consistent linear order for attribute lookup across the inheritance graph.

feedback.legit: It gives a consistent linear order for attribute lookup. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: It gives a consistent linear order for attribute lookup. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Python randomizes the base-class search on each call to avoid favoring one parent.

feedback.legit: Python computes a deterministic C3 linearization. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Python computes a deterministic C3 linearization. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

questions.prompt: A dataclass is marked `frozen=True` but contains a mutable list. How frozen is the object graph really?

answers.text: Completely frozen. Every list, dictionary, and nested object becomes immutable when stored in the dataclass.

feedback.legit: Frozen prevents normal field reassignment but does not freeze nested mutable objects. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Frozen prevents normal field reassignment but does not freeze nested mutable objects. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: The decorator deep-copies mutable constructor arguments, so outside references cannot change the stored values.

feedback.legit: Frozen dataclasses do not deep-copy their fields; mutable objects passed to the constructor remain mutable. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Frozen dataclasses do not deep-copy their fields; mutable objects passed to the constructor remain mutable. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: Frozen instances are always safely hashable, no matter which unhashable or mutable fields they contain.

feedback.legit: Generated hashing still depends on the dataclass options and on whether component values are hashable. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Generated hashing still depends on the dataclass options and on whether component values are hashable. The evidence supports your Sus vote.
